text: A mixture of glucose and fructose ingested at 2.4 g/min during prolonged cycling achieves peak exogenous carbohydrate oxidation rates of approximately 1.75 g/min, exceeding the ~1.0 g/min limit of glucose alone.
Ingesting glucose plus fructose at equal rates (1.2 g/min each) results in approximately 50% higher exogenous carbohydrate oxidation compared to ingesting glucose alone at the same total rate.
Glucose-only ingestion at 1.2 g/min yields peak exogenous oxidation rates of approximately 1.06 g/min, confirming an upper limit for single-transporter carbohydrate absorption.
specific_value: 1.06 g/min peak oxidation with glucose only

text: Combining glucose and fructose exploits separate intestinal transporters (SGLT1 and GLUT5), allowing total carbohydrate absorption and oxidation to exceed what either sugar achieves alone.

During sustained exercise at ~60% VO2max lasting 150 minutes, glucose-fructose mixtures maintain elevated exogenous oxidation rates (~1.49 g/min) over the final 90 minutes compared to glucose alone (~0.99 g/min).

For events lasting 2.5+ hours at moderate intensity, athletes should use glucose-fructose mixtures rather than glucose alone to maximize carbohydrate availability, targeting intake rates up to 2.4 g/min (144 g/hour).
